---
title: "Setup"
subtitle: "Day 0 — done on the evening of 23 July 2026. This page records what was installed, what you still need to do yourself, and how to rebuild any of it."
---

## Already done

| | Where |
|:--|:--|
| Python 3.12.13 virtual environment | `~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp` |
| PyTorch (CUDA 12.8 build) and the full stack | in that environment |
| Working repository | `~/Desktop/projects/llm-bootcamp-work` → [`sunami-lab/llm-bootcamp-work`](https://github.com/sunami-lab/llm-bootcamp-work) (private) |
| Karpathy reference repos | `~/Desktop/projects/llm-bootcamp-refs/` — `nn-zero-to-hero`, `micrograd`, `makemore`, `minbpe`, `nanoGPT` |

To start work on any day:

```bash
cd ~/Desktop/projects/llm-bootcamp-work
source env.sh
```

## Still yours to do — about five minutes

Two accounts, because they need a browser and a password you own:

- **[Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/join)** — needed from day 29. Create an access token under
  Settings → Access Tokens, then `huggingface-cli login`.
- **[Weights & Biases](https://wandb.ai/site)** — free for personal use, and it's what makes day 25's
  experiment table and the milestone write-ups easy. Then `wandb login`.

Optional: an API key for a hosted model (Anthropic or OpenAI) makes day 33's prompting baseline quicker.
An open instruction-tuned model from the Hub works too.

## The CUDA trap, and why the install is pinned

Your driver is **573.57, advertising CUDA 12.8**. The GPU is a GTX 1650 Ti (Turing, sm_75).

Installing `torch` from PyPI the obvious way gets you a **CUDA 13** build, which does not run on a 12.8
driver. It does not fail loudly either — it installs cleanly and then reports:

```
torch 2.13.0+cu130 | cuda available: False
```

which is the kind of thing you discover on day 25 when a sweep takes all afternoon on CPU. So the
environment pins the CUDA 12.8 build explicitly:

```bash
uv pip install --python ~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp/bin/python \
  --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu128 torch
```

If you ever rebuild the environment, use that line rather than a bare `uv pip install torch`.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
Sanity check before any GPU day: `python -c "import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available())"` must print
`True`. If it prints `False`, you are about to train on CPU.
:::

## Rebuilding the environment from scratch

```bash
uv venv ~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp --python 3.12
uv pip install --python ~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp/bin/python \
  --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu128 torch
uv pip install --python ~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp/bin/python \
  -r ~/Desktop/projects/llm-bootcamp-work/requirements.txt
```

The environment deliberately lives at `~/.venvs/llm-bootcamp`, on the Linux filesystem, rather than beside
the code under `/mnt/c`. WSL2's bridge to the Windows drive is slow for operations over thousands of small
files, which is exactly what a PyTorch venv is. Code and notebooks on `/mnt/c` are fine; the venv is not.

## What your 4 GB of VRAM will and won't do

This decides which days run on your laptop and which want Colab.

| Weeks | Locally? | Notes |
|:--|:--|:--|
| 1–4 | Comfortably | micrograd and makemore barely touch the GPU. The day 22–26 GPT is about 10.8 M parameters at the video's config and trains on TinyShakespeare in minutes. Day 25's sweeps are the first thing that really benefits from the card. |
| 5 | Tight but workable | A BERT-family encoder fine-tune fits with a modest batch size and mixed precision. Reach for gradient accumulation before reaching for Colab. |
| 6 | Depends | Day 41's LoRA run is where 4 GB starts choosing your model for you. Small model plus LoRA plus 4-bit fits; a full fine-tune of anything decoder-shaped probably doesn't. |
| 7 | Embeddings yes, speedrun no | Day 47's retrieval work runs locally. Day 45's nanochat speedrun needs 8×H100 — it is explicitly optional, and reading the pipeline carefully is the point. |

When something doesn't fit, [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/)'s free T4 has 16 GB and covers
everything in weeks 5–7.

## Verify

If this prints a decreasing loss and `cuda: True`, you are ready for day 1:

```python
import torch
print("cuda:", torch.cuda.is_available())

x = torch.randn(64, 3)
y = (x.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True) > 0).float()
w = torch.zeros(3, 1, requires_grad=True)
b = torch.zeros(1, requires_grad=True)

for step in range(200):
    loss = torch.nn.functional.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(x @ w + b, y)
    loss.backward()
    with torch.no_grad():
        w -= 0.5 * w.grad; b -= 0.5 * b.grad
        w.grad = None; b.grad = None
    if step % 50 == 0:
        print(step, round(loss.item(), 4))
```

You will not fully understand `loss.backward()` yet. That is days 2 through 4.

## Where things go

```
~/Desktop/projects/
├── llm-50-day-bootcamp/     this site, and data/schedule.yml
├── llm-bootcamp-work/       your notebooks, experiments and reports  ← work here
├── llm-bootcamp-refs/       Karpathy's repos, read-only reference
└── llm-bootcamp-prompts/    how this bootcamp was generated
```

Inside `llm-bootcamp-work`, name files by day —
`work/week-1-foundations/day-03-micrograd.ipynb` — so that on day 28 and day 50, when a milestone report
needs results from three weeks earlier, they are findable. `data/` and `checkpoints/` are gitignored:
commit the query, the seed and the split definition, not the data.
